topic: An employee is asked to train their replacement
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed, a grating soundtrack to my mounting frustration. Sarah, the manager, leaned across the desk, her smile stretched tight and brittle. "Look, Mark, I need you to run through the onboarding process for the new hire. You're the expert." I gripped the edge of my keyboard until my knuckles were white. Every word she spoke felt like an imposition, a deliberate undermining of my established competence. *This is ridiculous,* I thought, the heat rising in my chest. I could feel the familiar tightening in my jaw. "Fine," I managed, my voice coming out flatter than intended. "I'll do it." The sheer weight of having to repeat everything, to be reduced to a mere instructor, made my stomach clench with resentment.

[story 2]

I stared at the stack of manuals spread across my desk, the paper blurring slightly at the edges. When David called me in, his tone was overly cheerful, which only amplified the sour feeling bubbling up inside me. "So, you'll be mentoring Chloe now," he chirped. I pushed my chair back abruptly, the scrape of metal on linoleum echoing loudly in the quiet cubicle farm. I crossed my arms tightly over my chest, a defensive posture I couldn't shake. "I don't have time for this," I stated, my voice low and edged with sharp finality. I could see the flicker of surprise in his eyes, but I didn't offer an explanation. The urge to slam a file drawer shut was almost overwhelming. I just wanted the conversation to end.

[story 3]

The air in the training room felt thick, heavy with unspoken expectations. When the HR representative assigned me the task of briefing the incoming team member, a distinct tension settled over me. I shifted my weight from one foot to the other, a restless, jerky motion. When the new employee started asking questions, I responded with clipped, overly precise answers, every syllable delivered with an unnerving level of forced neutrality. Inside, a tight knot was constricting my throat. *Why me?* The thought was sharp and insistent. I wanted to snap at the simplest query, to let the sheer exasperation leak out, but I held it in, focusing instead on the precise angle of the projector screen, channeling the internal turmoil into rigid control.
